verses.fhb_text: Now the serpent was more crafty than any of the wild animals our Father had made. He said to the woman, "Did God really say, 'You must not eat from any tree in the garden'?"
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Establishing verse for the speaker-context exception — narrator names our Father (Genesis 1 anchor), serpent recasts him as distant "God."

Renderings:
• "our Father had made" — narrator's YHWH Elohim resolved per FHB-1.2-1 Genesis 1 anchor.
• "Did God really say" — serpent uses Elohim alone in distant register; preserved as "God" per FHB-2.9-1 + FHB-1.2-1 speaker-context exception.
• "You must not eat" — modal "shall" replaced with modern "must" per FHB-9.10-1.

Hebrew: vehannachash hayah arum (now the serpent was crafty) → "Now the serpent was more crafty" — FHB-9.3-1 conjunction variation, "Now" for scene shift.
verses.footnote: Having rejected our Father, the serpent reframes him as a distant, withholding deity — the foundational lie that hides the Father's heart from his children.

verses.fhb_text: The woman said to the serpent, "We may eat fruit from the trees in the garden,
verses.decision_notes: Eve answers truthfully about the garden's permission before slipping in v.3.

verses.fhb_text: but God has said, 'You must not eat fruit from the tree that is in the middle of the garden, and you must not touch it, or you will die.'"
verses.decision_notes: Eve repeats the command but slips into the serpent's distant-deity register — uses bare Elohim, not YHWH Elohim. Preserved as "God" per FHB-2.9-1 (the lie pattern beginning to take hold in her own mouth).

verses.fhb_text: "You will not surely die," the serpent said to the woman.
verses.decision_notes: Direct contradiction of our Father's word — the foundational lie. Hebrew lo-mot temutun preserves emphatic negation.

verses.fhb_text: "For God knows that on the day you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like God, knowing good and evil."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The serpent's lie distilled — "God" as withholding rule-giver rather than loving Father.

Renderings:
• "God knows" — Elohim alone, serpent's distant register per FHB-2.9-1.
• "like God" — predicative k'Elohim per FHB-1.2-1 speaker-context exception (the rule's own worked example).
verses.footnote: The serpent has been lying since Genesis 3:1 that God is a distant, unrelational deity — not the intimate Father he truly is.

verses.fhb_text: When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, pleasing to the eyes, and desirable for gaining wisdom, she took some of its fruit and ate. She also gave some to her husband, who was with her, and he ate.
verses.decision_notes: Narrative description; modern English rhythm with three parallel clauses preserved.

verses.fhb_text: Then the eyes of both of them were opened, and they realized they were naked; so they sewed fig leaves together and made coverings for themselves.
verses.decision_notes: First human attempt to cover their own shame — leaves they made themselves. Sets up the Father's better covering in v.21.

verses.fhb_text: Then they heard the sound of our Father walking in the garden in the cool of the day, and Adam and his wife hid from his presence among the trees of the garden.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Theophany — our Father walking with his children in intimate evening presence.

Renderings:
• "our Father walking" — first YHWH Elohim resolved per Genesis 1 anchor.
• "his presence" — second YHWH Elohim shifted to pronoun per FHB-9.2-1 (already established earlier in the verse).
• "Adam and his wife" — proper name per FHB-9.11-1, path continuity from Gen 2.

verses.fhb_text: But our Father called to Adam, "Where are you?"
verses.decision_notes: The Father seeks his children even in their hiding — "But" captures the irony of pursuing love against their flight.

verses.fhb_text: He answered, "I heard your voice in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked; so I hid."
verses.decision_notes: Adam's first words after the fall reveal the shape of sin: I heard, I was afraid, I hid. Hebrew et-qolekha shama'ti preserves qol-echo from v.8.

verses.fhb_text: And he said, "Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree I commanded you not to eat from?"
verses.decision_notes: Father speaks in first person ("I commanded") — preserve self-reference per FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 (no padded Father-language in first-person divine speech).

verses.fhb_text: The man said, "The woman you put here with me — she gave me some fruit from the tree, and I ate."
verses.decision_notes: Adam deflects responsibility to both the woman and the Father ("the woman you put here"). Em dash for parenthetical clarification per FHB-9.8-1 — single use, doing work commas cannot.

verses.fhb_text: Then our Father said to the woman, "What is this you have done?" The woman said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."
verses.decision_notes: YHWH Elohim → our Father per Genesis 1 anchor. The Father questions the woman face to face — relational accountability, not arbitrary judgment.

verses.fhb_text: So our Father said to the serpent, "Because you have done this, cursed are you above all livestock and all wild animals! You will crawl on your belly and eat dust all the days of your life."
verses.decision_notes: Father's judgment on the serpent — first curse in Scripture, on the one who had rejected him. Exclamation mark earned by the vocative force of the curse pronouncement per FHB-9.15-1.

verses.fhb_text: "And I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and hers; he will crush your head, and you will strike his heel."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The protoevangelium — first promise of the woman's offspring who will crush the serpent.

Renderings:
• "your offspring and hers" — zera rendered "offspring" per FHB-5.10-1 (warm familial; not archaic "seed").
• "he will crush your head, and you will strike his heel" — same Hebrew verb (shuf) rendered with English asymmetry to preserve the messianic decisiveness of the head-blow; NIV consensus per FHB-8.4-1.
verses.footnote: From the very first promise after the fall, our Father pointed to Jesus — the offspring who would crush the serpent and rescue his children.

verses.fhb_text: To the woman he said, "I will greatly increase your pains in childbearing; with painful labor you will give birth to children. Your desire will be for your husband, and he will rule over you."
verses.decision_notes: Father speaks the consequence to the woman. Hebrew hendiadys itzvonekh veheronekh → "pains in childbearing" per NIV/NLT/ESV consensus.

verses.fhb_text: To Adam he said, "Because you listened to your wife and ate from the tree about which I commanded you, 'You must not eat from it' — cursed is the ground because of you; through painful toil you will eat from it all the days of your life."
verses.decision_notes: Hebrew le'adam (without article) confirms proper-name reading per FHB-9.11-1. Em dash sets off the quoted command from the curse pronouncement per FHB-9.8-1.

verses.fhb_text: It will produce thorns and thistles for you, and you will eat the plants of the field.
verses.decision_notes: Continuation of the curse on the ground — the soil that was meant to nourish now resists.

verses.fhb_text: By the sweat of your brow you will eat your food until you return to the ground, since from it you were taken; for dust you are, and to dust you will return.
verses.decision_notes: Hebrew afar-afar parallel preserved — foundational mortality language echoed throughout Scripture (Eccl 3:20; 12:7).

verses.fhb_text: Adam named his wife Eve, because she would become the mother of all the living.
verses.decision_notes: Eve introduced at source's moment of naming per FHB-9.11-1. Hebrew Chavvah from chai (living) — Eve = "living one," mother of all chai. Adam's naming act echoes Gen 2:23, this time after the fall, still trusting our Father's life-promise.

verses.fhb_text: Our Father made garments of skin for Adam and his wife and clothed them.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: First picture of our Father covering his children's shame — better than the leaves they made for themselves.

Renderings:
• "Our Father made garments of skin" — YHWH Elohim resolved per Genesis 1 anchor; the Father-heart act of providing for his failed children.
• "clothed them" — Hebrew vayyalbishem preserves the tender Father-action; he doesn't just give them clothes, he dresses them.
verses.footnote: After their failure, our Father provides for his children a covering better than what they made for themselves — the first picture of his heart to clothe them with what only he can give.

verses.fhb_text: Then our Father said, "The man has now become like one of us, knowing good and evil. And now, he must not be allowed to reach out his hand and take also from the tree of life and eat, and live forever."
verses.decision_notes: Divine plural "like one of us" — Father speaks; the "us" includes Son and Holy Spirit per FHB-6.4.1-1(a). Preserved as "us" in body for first-person divine speech.

verses.fhb_text: So our Father sent him out of the Garden of Eden to work the ground from which he had been taken.
verses.decision_notes: Hebrew shalach (send out) — gentler verb; distinct from garash (drive out) in v.24. Distinction preserved per FHB-7.8-1.

verses.fhb_text: He drove the man out and placed cherubim on the east side of the Garden of Eden, and a flaming sword flashing back and forth to guard the way to the tree of life.
verses.decision_notes: Hebrew garash (drive out) — stronger verb than v.23's shalach; preserved distinction per FHB-7.8-1. Pronoun "he" carries path continuity from v.23 "our Father." Cherubim preserved as established English loanword per FHB-1.2-26.

chapter_notes: Genesis 3 is the foundational text for FHB-2.9-1 (the serpent's lie). The chapter's structural movement: (1) vv.1-5 — serpent recasts our Father as distant 'Elohim' who withholds, and Eve slips into the same distant register; (2) vv.6-7 — humanity acts on the lie and attempts self-covering with fig leaves; (3) vv.8-13 — the Father walks in the garden seeking his hiding children; (4) vv.14-19 — judgment includes the protoevangelium promise (v.15); (5) vv.20-21 — the Father covers his children with garments of skin, better than their fig leaves; (6) vv.22-24 — expulsion guards the way back to the tree of life. The narrator's voice resolves YHWH Elohim to 'our Father' throughout (Genesis 1 anchor per FHB-1.2-1); the serpent's and Eve's quoted speech preserves the distant 'God/Elohim' register per the speaker-context exception. Only one new footnote in this chapter — v.21 'father_clothes_shame' — because the other Father-heart and protoevangelium moments fall under categories already established in earlier chapters.
